stages: Introduce the learning target and ask students what good financial planning looks like.
Have students brainstorm what skills they need to manage money well.
Guide students to identify different levels of financial planning ability from beginner to expert.
Collaborate to define what each level looks like in practice.
Finalize the rubric language together and discuss how to use it.
student_prompts: What does it mean to plan your finances well?
What would someone who is just starting to learn about money management do differently from an expert?
How can we tell if our financial planning is getting better?
What tools or strategies help us manage money effectively?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show clear progression from simple list-making to complex financial scenarios with multiple tools and considerations.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to create shopping lists or calculate costs.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can create basic shopping lists with help but struggle with comparing prices or doing calculations.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can create shopping lists and compare prices on my own but sometimes make mistakes in calculations or savings plans.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can create shopping lists, compare prices accurately, calculate costs correctly, and make realistic savings plans using financial tools.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can apply my planning skills to complex situations and use multiple financial tools for comprehensive money management.

self_check_prompts: Can I create a shopping list and calculate the total cost without help?
Am I using appropriate tools to compare prices and plan my savings?

no_evidence: Provide a simple template for creating a basic shopping list.
Model how to identify and write down needed items.
emerging: Practice price comparison activities with guided support.
Use visual aids or calculators to support cost calculations.
developing: Review calculation strategies and provide practice with checking work.
competent: Present complex financial scenarios that require multiple planning steps.
Introduce advanced financial tools and apps for comprehensive planning.

stages: Students brainstorm what banking services they know and what financial needs people might have.
Students sort banking services into categories and discuss which needs each category addresses.
Students examine examples of different people's circumstances and match appropriate banking services.
Students create criteria for what makes a good analysis of banking service alignment with needs.
student_prompts: What banking services have you heard of and what do you think they help people do?
How might a student's banking needs differ from a family with young children or someone starting retirement?
What should we look for when deciding if someone has done a good job analyzing banking services for different people?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work that shows clear progression from basic service identification to sophisticated analysis connecting services with specific circumstances and needs.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to analyse banking services.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some banking services with help but my analysis needs improvement.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify banking services and try to analyse them but miss some important connections to people's needs.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can analyse how different banking services match with various people's financial needs and circumstances.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can apply my analysis to new banking situations or connect it with broader financial planning.

self_check_prompts: Have I explained how specific banking services meet particular financial needs?
Did I consider different people's circumstances when choosing appropriate services?

no_evidence: Provide a simple banking scenario and ask what services might help.
Offer a choice between two banking services and ask which seems more useful.
emerging: Guide student to check their service identification against a reference list.
Ask student to explain one connection between a service and a specific need.
developing: Prompt student to consider what circumstances make certain services more or less suitable.
Ask student to identify one missing connection between services and needs in their analysis.
competent: Challenge student to apply their analysis framework to a completely new banking scenario.
Encourage student to connect banking service choices with longer-term financial goals.

stages: Brainstorm what exchange means and collect student examples from daily life
Sort examples into different types of exchange systems
Explore why people specialize in certain jobs or skills
Connect specialization to the need for trade and exchange
Refine understanding by identifying quality indicators for explanations
student_prompts: What are some ways people get things they need from others?
Why don't people make everything they need themselves?
How does someone becoming really good at one thing affect what they need from others?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work that shows clear progression from simple identification of exchanges to sophisticated connections between specialization and trade systems.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to explain exchange or specialisation

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some exchanges with help but my explanations need work

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can explain basic exchanges and recognise specialisation but struggle to connect them to trade

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how people exchange goods, services, and resources through different systems and why specialisation leads to trade

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can connect exchange systems and specialisation to broader economic patterns or historical developments

self_check_prompts: Can I clearly explain why specialisation creates the need for trade?
Have I described specific exchange systems and how they work?

no_evidence: Provide concrete examples of exchanges from student's daily life
Use visual organizers to map simple trade relationships
emerging: Ask students to explain their thinking about specific exchanges
Provide sentence starters for clearer explanations
developing: Guide students to trace the connection from specialisation to trade needs
Use questioning to help students link their examples together
competent: Challenge students to consider historical or global examples
Ask how exchange systems might change over time or in different contexts

stages: Start by having students brainstorm all the jobs they know from their community and families.
Guide students to sort these jobs into groups based on what workers actually do.
Help students discuss why people work and what happens because of their work.
Lead students to create descriptions of what good understanding looks like at different levels.
student_prompts: What jobs do people in your family or neighborhood do?
How does this person's work help other people or make money?
What would happen if no one did this type of work?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose work examples from students' direct experience like teachers, store workers, or family members that clearly show helping others or earning money.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to name jobs or explain why people work.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some jobs with help but need support explaining what they do.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can name different types of work but my explanations about helping others or earning money need more details.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can identify different kinds of work people do and explain how their work helps others or earns money.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can connect different types of work to show how communities depend on each other and how money flows through our economy.

self_check_prompts: Can I name specific jobs and explain what those workers do?
Can I tell how this work helps other people or makes money for the worker?

no_evidence: Provide picture cards of familiar community workers to start naming jobs.
Ask about jobs the student sees every day at school or home.
emerging: Use sentence starters like 'This worker helps people by...' to scaffold explanations.
Connect jobs to student experiences by asking how specific workers have helped them.
developing: Push for specific details by asking 'How exactly does this help people?' or 'Where does their money come from?'
Encourage students to think about what would happen if this job didn't exist.
competent: Challenge students to trace connections between different jobs in their community.
Ask how one type of work depends on or supports other types of work.

stages: Students brainstorm different types of work they know about.
Students discuss what might influence someone's choice of work.
Students examine examples of work comparisons and identify what makes them strong.
Students draft criteria for comparing work types and analyzing influences.
Students refine criteria into rubric levels through peer feedback.
student_prompts: What different types of work do you see in your community?
What factors might make someone choose one job over another?
How can we tell if someone has done a good job comparing different types of work?
What would it look like to analyze work influences deeply versus just listing them?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select work comparison examples that show clear progression from simple identification to complex analysis of multiple interconnected influences.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to compare work types or analyze what influences work choices.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some work types with help but my comparisons are incomplete or inaccurate.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can independently compare basic work types and identify obvious influences but lack depth.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently compare different types of work and analyze multiple factors that influence people's work choices.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can transfer my analysis to complex scenarios and evaluate how work influences connect to each other comprehensively.

self_check_prompts: Have I compared multiple types of work clearly?
Have I analyzed several different factors that influence work choices?
Can I explain how these influences connect to each other?

no_evidence: Provide examples of different work types to get started.
Ask what they notice about jobs in their family or community.
emerging: Guide them to focus on one clear comparison between two work types.
Help them identify one specific influence on work choice.
developing: Encourage them to explore less obvious influences beyond money and location.
Push them to explain why certain influences matter more than others.
competent: Challenge them to apply their analysis to unfamiliar work scenarios.
Ask them to explore how different influences interact with each other.

stages: Brainstorm what students already know about credit and borrowing from personal experience.
Examine the key components that make credit systems work.
Explore how borrowing creates debt obligations and responsibilities.
Define what quality explanations of credit-debt relationships look like.
Create success criteria for connecting credit concepts to real situations.
student_prompts: What happens when someone uses a credit card or takes out a loan?
What are the essential parts of any credit system?
How does borrowing money create obligations for the borrower?
What makes an explanation of credit clear and complete?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student explanations that show different levels of understanding credit mechanics and varying abilities to connect borrowing with debt responsibilities.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to explain how credit or debt systems work.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some features of credit with help but my explanations need work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can explain basic credit concepts on my own but struggle to clearly describe how borrowing creates debt.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently explain how credit systems work and accurately describe the relationship between borrowing and debt obligations.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can connect credit and debt concepts to broader financial systems and real-world applications.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain the key parts of credit systems without help?
Can I clearly describe what happens when someone borrows money and what obligations they have?

no_evidence: Provide examples of common credit situations like credit cards or loans.
Ask what the student knows about borrowing money from family or friends.
emerging: Guide the student to identify one key feature of credit systems accurately.
Provide sentence starters for explaining basic credit concepts.
developing: Help the student connect specific borrowing actions to specific debt obligations.
Ask the student to explain what responsibilities come with borrowing.
competent: Challenge the student to apply credit concepts to new financial situations.
Ask how credit systems connect to other parts of the economy.

stages: Students share what they know about budgets and financial planning from their own experience.
Class identifies the key components that make financial planning effective through discussion.
Students examine what makes financial decisions 'informed' versus random or impulsive.
Groups brainstorm different levels of skill from beginner to expert in using financial tools.
Class refines and agrees on criteria for each performance level.
student_prompts: What financial planning have you seen adults do in your life?
What information would you need to make a good financial decision?
How would you know if someone is getting better at managing money?
What's the difference between using one tool versus combining multiple tools?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work samples that clearly show progression from basic budget identification to independent tool use to sophisticated integration of multiple planning approaches.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to use any financial planning tools.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify basic parts of a budget with help but my planning is incomplete.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can create simple budgets on my own but struggle to use tools for bigger financial decisions.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can use budgets and financial planning tools independently to make informed decisions about spending, saving, and goals.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can combine multiple financial planning tools to optimize complex decisions and compare different options.

self_check_prompts: Can I create and use financial planning tools without help?
Do my financial decisions show clear thinking about spending, saving, and goals?

no_evidence: Provide a simple budget template and guide student through identifying income and expenses.
Model one financial decision using basic planning steps.
emerging: Help student practice completing budget categories independently.
Guide student to check their work for accuracy and completeness.
developing: Encourage student to apply their budgeting skills to a specific financial goal or decision.
Introduce one additional planning tool beyond basic budgeting.
competent: Challenge student to compare multiple financial options using their planning tools.
Introduce more complex scenarios requiring integration of different planning approaches.
